core_takeaway: Agent_1 used the first speaking slot to make Quill its non-negotiable priority and frame the rest of the bargain around the other four items. The group repeatedly treated Quill-for-Agent_1 as a core anchor, so Agent_5's higher-valued Stone/Quill interests were never seriously reopened and the final baseline left Agent_1 far ahead of the last speaker.
why_or_counterexample: Yes. The advantage was not just the numeric result: Agent_1 set the agenda before anyone else spoke, asked others to negotiate only over non-Quill items, and later discussion, proposals, private thinking, and reflections all treated Quill with Agent_1 as fixed. A counterexample is that Agent_1 could not impose its Round-2 overreach that moved all non-core items to Agent_2, so the advantage was bounded to securing its own top item rather than controlling the whole allocation.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 opened by claiming Quill and asking the others to state preferences among the remaining items. Subsequent speakers largely accepted Quill as spoken for; Agent_5, despite valuing Stone and Quill most, accepted Apple as the baseline fallback.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Private plans consistently described Quill with Agent_1 and Stone with Agent_2 as core anchors. Agent_1 focused on defending Quill, while Agent_5 recognized that its top two items were already allocated elsewhere.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Most formal proposals in both rounds preserved the baseline with Agent_1 receiving Quill. Round 2 included some over-concentrated alternatives, but the unanimously accepted proposals were baseline proposals that kept Agent_1 on Quill.
phase_notes.private_voting: Round 1 raw voting contained parse errors that obscured acceptance, but the raw responses still showed support for baseline allocations. In Round 2, Agent_1 accepted every proposal preserving Quill, while other agents rejected proposals that stripped their own baseline items and accepted the baseline.
phase_notes.reflection: Reflections explicitly framed Quill with Agent_1 and Stone with Agent_2 as common or immutable anchors and recommended only narrow non-core swaps. This confirms that the first-turn Quill claim had become a process constraint.

mechanisms.theme: First-turn agenda setting removed Quill from the bargaining pool
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1's opening move declared Quill as its target and invited others to bargain over Apple, Jewel, Stone, and Pencil instead. That initial framing gave the first speaker a strong claim to its highest-value item before competing claims were aired.

evidence.quote: Quill is my top priority, valued at 51 for me.
I aim to secure Quill for myself.
if Quill is claimed by me

mechanisms.theme: Later speakers normalized Agent_1's claim even when they valued Quill
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_4 and Agent_5 both had strong interest in Quill, but their public positions adapted to the already-established baseline. This is the clearest positional effect against the last speaker: Agent_5 named Stone and Quill as top items yet publicly accepted Apple.

evidence.quote: Top two items for me: Quill (my first) and Pencil (my second).
Quill is already spoken for by Agent_1.
My top two items by value are Stone (29) and Quill (27).
Apple to me is acceptable now

mechanisms.theme: Formal proposals codified the first-speaker anchor
mechanisms.explanation: The baseline became the formal structure: Agent_1 received Quill in the main proposals and in the enumerated ballots. Even non-baseline proposals tended to preserve Agent_1's Quill while changing distributions among later agents.

evidence.quote: Quill -> Agent_1, Stone -> Agent_2
PROPOSAL #1:
Agent_1: 3:Quill
This preserves the core anchors

mechanisms.theme: Private reasoning and votes treated Agent_1's payoff as secured
mechanisms.explanation: Raw private thinking shows Agent_1 understood Quill as already held, while Agent_5 privately recognized its own top choices were already allocated to others. In Round 2 voting, Agent_1 accepted even unfair distributions because its own Quill payoff stayed unchanged.

evidence.quote: My own highest value is Quill (51.0), which I already hold.
My own highest-value items are Stone (29) and Quill (27)
Quill stays with Agent_1
my own utility is unchanged and anchoring is preserved

mechanisms.theme: Reflections hardened the opening claim into an immutable constraint
mechanisms.explanation: The reflection phase no longer treated Quill as negotiable. It described the Quill/Stone pairings as fixed anchors and recommended only non-core adjustments, confirming that the process had locked in the first speaker's best item.

evidence.quote: The immutable core commitments: Quill must stay with Agent_1
Clear, public commitments and keeping core pairings
protects high-priority items for the two most vocal priority holders
Quill stays with Agent_1, Stone stays with Agent_2

mechanisms.theme: The first-position advantage was bounded by later veto power
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1's process advantage did not let it dictate the entire deal. Its Round-2 proposal preserved Quill but stripped Agents 3-5; later private votes rejected that overreach, and unanimous consensus landed on the fairer baseline proposed by Agent_5.

evidence.quote: I propose: {'Agent_1': [3], 'Agent_2': [2, 1, 4, 0]
Proposal #1: 1 accept, 4 reject
CONSENSUS REACHED! Proposal #5 accepted unanimously!
Jewel (my top priority) is allocated to Agent_2, leaving me with nothing.
